data.title: Dr. Oz Says Like It Or Not, AI Avatars Are 'Best Way' To Help Fix Health Care — Robots Do Ultrasounds And 'Frankly, I Don't Have To See The Image'
data.description:
A doctor, a robot, and a rural hospital walk into a policy debate. Only one of them might actually be in the room.

"There's no question about it — whether you want it or not — the best way to help some of these communities is gonna be AI-based avatars," Oz said. He added that the technology could multiply the reach of doctors fivefold or more without burning them out.

Don't Miss:

The proposal lands at a moment when access to care is already slipping out of reach for many Americans, especially outside major cities.

Rural Health Care Is Shrinking Fast

Maternity care has been hit especially hard. Labor and delivery units have been disappearing at a steady clip, leaving some counties without any local options for childbirth. For many families, that means long drives for routine care or emergencies, or in some cases, skipping care altogether.

Federal data adds another layer. A report from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in 2024 found that preventable premature deaths remain higher in rural areas than in urban ones across major causes such as heart disease, cancer, and stroke. The gap highlights a system already under strain before any new technology enters the picture.

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

A $50 Billion Bet On Technology

Oz's comments tie into a broader federal push. The Trump administration's Rural Health Transformation Program, created under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, sets aside $50 billion to modernize care in underserved areas. The plan spreads funding over several years, with states already beginning to roll out pilot programs.

Some of those programs lean heavily on the kind of tools Oz described. Digital avatars could handle basic patient interviews. Remote monitoring systems could track chronic conditions. In more experimental cases, drones may deliver medications in areas without nearby pharmacies, and robotic systems could assist with diagnostics from miles away.

Oz pointed to one of the more striking examples when discussing maternal care.

"We can use robots to do ultrasounds on pregnant women," Oz said told Axios in January. "You take a wand, you don't even see the image—you just get digitized insights that tell you whether the child's OK. And frankly, I don't have to see the image. I just have to know if the image is good enough to tell me the child doesn't have a problem."

Critics Question The Trade-Offs

Not everyone is sold on the idea that more technology is the answer. Some policymakers and health experts argue that the focus should remain on staffing and infrastructure rather than automation.

There are practical hurdles as well. Many rural communities still face gaps in broadband access, which could limit the effectiveness of remote care tools. Patient trust is another factor. For some, the idea of replacing in-person visits with digital avatars may feel less like progress and more like a downgrade.

Extending Care Or Replacing It

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services has since clarified that the goal is not to replace doctors but to extend their reach. In a statement to NPR, the agency said it supports using AI tools when they are evidence-based, patient-centered, and deployed under proper clinical oversight.

Oz has continued to lean into that message. At a health conference earlier this month, he spoke about expanding AI-driven tools across Medicare, framing them as a way to guide patients, monitor conditions, and reduce hospital visits. He also acknowledged that skepticism remains, suggesting that both patients and providers will need time to adjust.

The broader question is still open. Technology may help fill gaps, but whether it can do so without losing the human side of care is something the system has yet to prove.

As artificial intelligence takes on a larger role in high-stakes decisions—from health care to diagnostics—some investors are also exploring how AI can support financial decision-making. Platforms like Public have introduced AI-powered tools designed to help users analyze investments and build portfolios, reflecting a broader shift toward machine-assisted guidance across industries.

Image: Imagn

data.title: Dow Chief Warns Iran War Petrochemical Shock Could Take 275 Days To Clear After Hormuz Strait Reopens: 'The Die Is Being Cast For...'
data.description:
The Iran war has already set the stage for elevated petrochemical prices through year-end, and the supply chain damage could take the better part of a year to unwind, Dow chairman and CEO Jim Fitterling said.

Speaking at the CERAWeek by S&P Global conference in Houston on Thursday, Fitterling warned that nearly 20% of global petrochemical capacity is now effectively blocked by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, with ripple effects expected across construction materials, consumer goods, and the automotive and aerospace industries.

“The die is being cast for the rest of the year,” Fitterling said, comparing the disruption to the COVID-era supply chain snags. He estimated a 250- to 275-day recovery window once the strait reopens, cautioning it would not be an immediate rebound.

While roughly 150 vessels typically flow through the Strait of Hormuz each day, Fitterling estimates only about 15 escorted ships will initially proceed daily when the strait eventually reopens — with oil and gas tankers prioritized first, then fertilizer, with petrochemicals likely further down the queue.

East Vs West

Fitterling noted that U.S. petrochemical plants run largely on ethane — a natural gas derivative unaffected by the war — while Asian and European plants depend on naphtha, nearly half of Asia’s supply of which flows through the strait. That pricing gap between the U.S. and Asia, typically under $500 per metric ton, has already surged above $1,200.

The supply shock will exacerbate the so-called K-shaped economic trends, he said, and create greater haves and have-nots between the Western and Eastern hemispheres.

Meanwhile, despite Dow’s stock climbing nearly 70% year to date, Fitterling said he is not celebrating. He warned that the war’s inflationary impact could push interest rates higher, dampening housing demand and broader economic growth.

“The volatility is off the charts right now,” he said.

Four weeks into the U.S.-Israel-Iran war, thousands of people have been killed as tensions continue to escalate, and soaring energy prices fuel inflation fears.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Why Is Rocket Pharma Stock Soaring Friday?
data.description:
FDA Accelerated Approval Drives Investor Sentiment

This significant development is boosting investor sentiment as the company aims to enhance financial flexibility through the monetization of a Rare Pediatric Disease Priority Review Voucher granted by the FDA, which could further support its growth strategy.

The first FDA-approved gene therapy for this ultra-rare condition underscores Rocket’s commitment to addressing critical pediatric health needs.

How Kresladi Works

Kresladi is an autologous hematopoietic stem cell-based gene therapy that treats severe LAD-I, a rare, inherited immune deficiency caused by mutations in the ITGB2 gene that prevent white blood cells from effectively fighting infections.

Kresladi uses the patient’s own hematopoietic (blood) stem cells (HSCs), which clinicians genetically modify to introduce functional copies of the ITGB2 gene.

After conditioning, clinicians infuse a single intravenous dose of Kresladi to address the underlying cause of severe LAD-I by restoring CD18 and CD11a cell surface expression in white blood cells, including neutrophils.

Clinical Data Shows Strong Survival And Safety Profile

The approval is supported by positive clinical efficacy and safety data from the global Phase 1/2 study of Kresladi, which demonstrated 100% overall survival at 12 months post-infusion (and for the entire duration of follow-up) for all enrolled patients.

All primary and secondary endpoints were met, and Kresladi was well tolerated in all patients with no treatment-related serious adverse events.

The data showed substantial reductions in the incidence of significant infections compared to pre-treatment levels, along with evidence of improvement in skin lesions and restoration of wound-healing capabilities.

Technical Analysis

Currently, Rocket Pharmaceuticals is trading 12.15% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 39.7% above its 100-day SMA, demonstrating a strong short-term bullish trend.

Shares have decreased by 40.48% over the past 12 months and are currently positioned closer to their 52-week highs than lows, suggesting a recovery phase in the stock’s price action.

The RSI is at 55.68, which is considered neutral territory, indicating that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold at this time.

Meanwhile, MACD is at 0.1168, below its signal line at 0.1807, indicating bearish pressure on the stock.

The combination of neutral RSI and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum, indicating that while the stock is showing some strength, there are underlying bearish signals that traders should monitor closely.

  • Key Resistance: $5.50
  • Key Support: $4.50

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $32.25. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Goldman Sachs: Sell (Raises Target to $3.00) (Mar. 2)
  • Cantor Fitzgerald: Overweight (Raises Target to $10.00) (Feb. 27)

RCKT Stock Price Activity: Rocket Pharmaceuticals shares were up 10.66% at $5.19 during premarket trading on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: What's Going On With Alphabet Stock Friday?
data.description:
The tech giant faces a combination of courtroom setbacks and broader macroeconomic pressures. Nasdaq futures are down 0.60% while S&P 500 futures have shed 0.39%.

Legal Defeats Impact Sentiment

The verdict notably bypassed the Section 230 legal shield.

Macroeconomic Pressures Weigh In

Meanwhile, the 10-year Treasury bond yield sits at 4.45%, keeping growth stocks under a microscope.

Technical Analysis

Alphabet is trading 8.6% below its 20-day SMA and 10.9% below its 100-day SMA, showing the short-to-intermediate trend has weakened even as the longer-term uptrend is still technically intact with shares 5.4% above the 200-day SMA.

Shares are up 73.15% over the past 12 months, but the stock is now positioned closer to its 52-week lows than highs after failing to hold the February breakout.

RSI is at 27.56, which puts the stock in oversold territory and often signals selling pressure may be getting stretched. Meanwhile, MACD is at -5.8217 and below its signal line at -4.0757, keeping bearish momentum in control for now.

The combination of oversold RSI (below 30) and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum.

  • Key Resistance: $312.50
  • Key Support: $270.50

Price Action: Alphabet’s GOOGL was down 1.08% to $277.89, while GOOG was down 1.27% at $277.40 in Friday’s premarket, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo by Markus Mainka via Shutterstock
